title: Phishing: The Digital Bait 🎣
description: Craft convincing emails, clone websites, and bypass email security controls.

slides.title: What is Phishing? 📧
slides.content:
# Casting a Wide Net

**Phishing** is a fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information (usernames, passwords, credit card details) by disguising oneself as a trustworthy entity in electronic communication.

**Types of Phishing:**
- **Mass Phishing**: Generic emails sent to thousands. (e.g., *"Your Apple ID has been locked."*)
- **Spear Phishing**: Highly targeted at a specific individual or department.
- **Whaling**: Targeting high-profile executives (CEO, CFO).
- **Clone Phishing**: Replacing a legitimate link/attachment in a real email with a malicious one.

**Analogy**: Mass phishing is fishing with a huge net in the ocean. Spear phishing is using a spear gun to catch one specific, valuable fish.

quizData.question: An attacker researches the CFO of a company and sends a personalized email referencing a recent conference they both attended. This is an example of:
quizData.explanation: Spear phishing is targeted and personalized using OSINT.

slides.title: Anatomy of a Phishing Email ✉️
slides.content:
# Crafting the Perfect Bait

**1. The Sender (From:)**
- **Spoofing**: Forging the `From:` header (e.g., `ceo@company.com`). 
- **Look-alike Domains**: `micros0ft.com` instead of `microsoft.com`, or `amazon-support.net`.
- **Display Name Spoofing**: `"IT Helpdesk" <hacker123@gmail.com>`.

**2. The Subject Line**
- Creates **urgency** or **curiosity**.
- *"ACTION REQUIRED: Password Expires in 2 Hours"*
- *"Voice Message from CEO (1:23)"*

**3. The Body**
- Logos and corporate branding (copied from real emails).
- Professional language and grammar (AI tools like ChatGPT make this trivial now).
- A clear **Call to Action (CTA)** : *"Click Here to Verify Account"*.

**4. The Payload**
- **Link**: Points to a **credential harvester** (fake login page).
- **Attachment**: Malicious Office document with macros or a PDF with an embedded link.

slides.title: Cloning Websites (Credential Harvesting) 🎭
slides.content:
# Building the Fake Login Page

**Tool: SET (Social-Engineer Toolkit)**
Included in Kali, SET automates the cloning of websites and the harvesting of credentials.

**Workflow with SET:**
1.  `sudo setoolkit`
2.  Select **1) Social-Engineering Attacks**.
3.  Select **2) Website Attack Vectors**.
4.  Select **3) Credential Harvester Attack Method**.
5.  Select **2) Site Cloner**.
6.  Enter your **Kali IP** (for the POST back).
7.  Enter the **URL to clone** (e.g., `https://login.microsoftonline.com`).

SET will host a fake login page. When the victim enters their credentials, they are **captured** and displayed in your terminal, and the victim is then redirected to the real site (so they don't get suspicious).

**Note**: You need to send the phishing email containing the link to **your** Kali IP.

slides.title: SET Credential Harvester in Action 💻
slides.content: Example of running SET to clone a login page.
slides.codeSnippet:
sudo setoolkit

# Menu Navigation:
1 (Social-Engineering Attacks)
2 (Website Attack Vectors)
3 (Credential Harvester Attack Method)
2 (Site Cloner)

# Set IP address for POST back:
IP address for the POST back in Harvester/Tabnabbing: 192.168.56.102

# Enter URL to clone:
Enter the url to clone: https://example.com/login

# Press Enter to start. Visit http://192.168.56.102 in a browser to see the clone.

slides.title: Bypassing Email Security (SPF/DKIM/DMARC) 🛡️
slides.content:
# Why Spoofing Fails (And How to Work Around It)

Modern email servers check three DNS records to prevent spoofing.

| Record | Purpose | Attacker Bypass |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SPF** | Lists IPs allowed to send mail for the domain. | Use **look-alike domains** (`rnicrosoft.com`). |
| **DKIM** | Cryptographic signature verifying email integrity. | Hard to bypass; focus on look-alike domains. |
| **DMARC** | Tells server what to do if SPF/DKIM fails. | If policy is `p=none`, spoofing works! Check with `dig _dmarc.domain.com TXT`. |

**The Real-World Strategy**: **Do not spoof the exact domain.** Register a **typosquat domain**:
- `microsotf.com`
- `amazonsupport.com`
- `paypa1.com`

Set up proper SPF/DKIM for **that fake domain** so it lands in the Inbox, not Spam.

quizData.question: You run `dig _dmarc.company.com TXT` and see `v=DMARC1; p=none;`. What does this mean for an attacker?

options.text: The domain is highly secure; spoofing is impossible.
Spoofed emails might be delivered because the policy is set to monitor only (no action).

quizData.explanation: `p=none` means DMARC is in monitoring mode. It does not reject or quarantine failing emails.

slides.title: Advanced: HTML Smuggling & QR Code Phishing (Quishing) 📦
slides.content:
# Evolving Techniques

**HTML Smuggling**
Instead of attaching a `.exe` (blocked), you attach an **HTML file** that, when opened, **reconstructs the malicious file** locally using JavaScript. This bypasses attachment filters.

**Quishing (QR Code Phishing)**
Attach a QR code to the email. The user scans it with their **personal phone** (outside corporate email security). The phone browser then opens the malicious link.

**MFA Fatigue (Push Bombing)**
If you have the user's password (from a previous breach), you spam them with MFA push notifications until they accidentally approve one or approve just to stop the noise.

slides.title: Phishing Simulation Ethics ⚠️
slides.content:
## Never Collect Real Passwords

In a professional phishing simulation, **never harvest and store real user passwords**. Use a fake login page that simply displays 'This was a simulation' and tracks the click. Storing real credentials violates privacy and security policies.
